one_liner: A 45-year-old woman presents with 6 months of progressive fatigue, cold intolerance, constipation, dry skin, 8-pound weight gain despite a poor appetite, and a painless goiter.

stage2.hpi_extension: She feels sluggish, sleeps 10 hours a night but still feels unrested, and wears a sweater in a warm office. Her skin has become dry and her hair is thinning. She has heavy menses (menorrhagia) and mild depression. She noticed a gradual painless enlargement of the front of her neck over months. She denies tremor, palpitations, heat intolerance, or eye symptoms. No neck pain, no recent viral illness.
stage2.family_history: Mother with hypothyroidism; sister with type 1 diabetes
stage2.physical_exam: General: tired-appearing, puffy. HEENT: **periorbital puffiness**, coarse dry skin, thinning eyebrows (lateral third). Neck: **diffusely enlarged, firm, non-tender ('rubbery') goiter** with a bumpy/bosselated surface; no bruit. CV: **bradycardic**, regular. Resp: clear. Abdomen: soft, non-tender. Extremities: **non-pitting edema**. Skin: dry, cool. Neuro: **delayed relaxation phase of deep tendon reflexes** ('hung-up' reflexes).

lab_results.result: **TSH markedly elevated (22 mIU/L)**. **Free T4 low (0.6 ng/dL)**. Free T3 low-normal. Prolactin mildly elevated.
lab_results.clinical_significance: Elevated TSH with low free T4 confirms primary hypothyroidism. TRH-driven prolactin elevation is a common incidental finding. In an adult with goiter and hypothyroidism, autoimmune (Hashimoto) thyroiditis is the leading cause in iodine-sufficient regions.

lab_results.result: **Anti-thyroid peroxidase (anti-TPO) antibodies markedly positive**. **Anti-thyroglobulin antibodies positive**. TSI (thyroid stimulating immunoglobulin) negative.
lab_results.clinical_significance: Positive anti-TPO and anti-thyroglobulin antibodies are the serologic hallmark of Hashimoto thyroiditis. Negative TSI excludes Graves disease. Hashimoto is associated with HLA-DR3 and HLA-DR5.

lab_results.result: **LDL cholesterol elevated**, **hyponatremia (Na 132)**, mildly elevated creatine kinase. Fasting glucose normal.
lab_results.clinical_significance: Hypothyroidism causes hyperlipidemia (decreased LDL receptor expression), mild hyponatremia (reduced free water clearance), and mild CK elevation from myopathy. These peripheral findings support longstanding hypothyroidism.

stage5.final_clue: She is started on levothyroxine. Over 6 weeks her TSH begins to normalize, energy improves, and the goiter shrinks modestly. She is counseled that Hashimoto is a lifelong disease with a small long-term risk of primary thyroid lymphoma.

debrief.classic_presentation: Hashimoto thyroiditis is the most common cause of hypothyroidism and goiter in iodine-sufficient areas. Middle-aged women present with fatigue, cold intolerance, weight gain, constipation, dry skin, brittle hair, menorrhagia, bradycardia, periorbital puffiness, non-pitting edema, and a painless 'rubbery' diffusely enlarged goiter. Delayed relaxation ('hung-up') of deep tendon reflexes is a classic bedside sign. Associations with other autoimmune diseases (type 1 DM, celiac, vitiligo, Addison, pernicious anemia) are common.
debrief.pathophysiology: Chronic autoimmune (T-cell and antibody-mediated) destruction of the thyroid gland. Histology shows a dense lymphocytic infiltrate with germinal centers and Hurthle cells (eosinophilic epithelial cells). Serologic hallmarks are anti-thyroid peroxidase (anti-TPO) and anti-thyroglobulin antibodies. Strong HLA-DR3 and HLA-DR5 associations. There is a small but increased lifetime risk of primary thyroid (B-cell, MALT-type) lymphoma.
debrief.treatment_overview: Lifelong levothyroxine (synthetic T4) replacement, titrated to keep TSH within the normal range. Start low and uptitrate in elderly or cardiac patients to avoid precipitating arrhythmia/ischemia. Monitor TSH approximately every 6-8 weeks until stable. In pregnancy, thyroid hormone requirements rise (~30%) and TSH targets are stricter. Subclinical hypothyroidism (high TSH, normal T4) is often treated if TSH >10 or if symptomatic, pregnant, or anti-TPO positive.
high_yield_pearls: Hashimoto thyroiditis: most common cause of hypothyroidism and goiter in iodine-sufficient countries.
Serologic hallmark: anti-TPO and anti-thyroglobulin antibodies; HLA-DR3 and HLA-DR5 associations.
Histology: lymphocytic infiltrate with germinal centers and Hurthle cells (eosinophilic metaplasia).
Delayed ('hung-up') relaxation of deep tendon reflexes is a classic exam finding in hypothyroidism.
Increased risk of primary thyroid B-cell lymphoma (MALT) in Hashimoto patients.
sources: First Aid for the USMLE Step 1 (2017) — Endocrine chapter, 'Hypothyroidism — Hashimoto thyroiditis' section.
